text:
CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION:
58-year-old postmenopausal Hispanic woman with newly diagnosed left breast mass, biopsy-proven invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, triple-negative subtype (ER negative, PR negative, HER2 1+ by IHC, not amplified by FISH). Staging examination to evaluate for distant metastatic disease prior to treatment planning. Known left axillary lymphadenopathy by prior ultrasound.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed with intravenous contrast (100 mL of iohexol 350 administered via antecubital vein at 3 mL/sec) in the portal venous phase for abdomen and pelvis, with dedicated lung windows and mediastinal windows for the chest. Axial images were acquired from lung apices to pubic symphysis with coronal and sagittal reformats. No prior CT available for direct comparison at this institution. Oral contrast was not administered.

FINDINGS:

LUNGS AND AIRWAYS:
The lungs are well-aerated bilaterally. No focal pulmonary nodules, masses, or consolidation identified. No pleural effusions. No pneumothorax. The central airways are patent without endobronchial lesion. The trachea is midline.

PLEURA:
No pleural thickening or pleural effusion bilaterally.

MEDIASTINUM AND HILA:
The heart is normal in size. There is no pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta and pulmonary vasculature are unremarkable in caliber. No mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes are identified meeting size criteria for pathologic enlargement (all visualized nodes are less than 1.0 cm in short axis). No anterior mediastinal mass.

CHEST WALL:
A spiculated, irregular soft tissue density corresponding to the known primary left breast mass is identified at the 12 o'clock position, consistent with the known diagnosis. This examination is not optimally suited for breast lesion characterization; dedicated breast imaging is on record. The chest wall musculature and osseous structures of the thorax are intact without aggressive bone lesion.

LEFT AXILLA:
An enlarged left axillary lymph node is again noted, measuring approximately 1.9 cm in long axis with loss of normal fatty hilum and cortical thickening. This finding is consistent with the previously described lymphadenopathy on the recent breast ultrasound and represents the known locoregional nodal involvement. No additional enlarged axillary nodes are identified. The right axilla is unremarkable.

RIGHT BREAST:
No definite suspicious mass or axillary lymphadenopathy on the right side within the limitations of CT for breast evaluation.

LIVER:
The liver is normal in size with smooth margins. Hepatic parenchyma demonstrates homogeneous attenuation without focal hypodense or hyperdense lesions to suggest hepatic metastases, cysts, or hemangiomas on this contrast-enhanced examination. The intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. The portal and hepatic veins are patent.

GALLBLADDER AND BILIARY SYSTEM:
The gallbladder is present and without wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, or calculi. No biliary ductal dilatation.

SPLEEN:
Normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesion.

PANCREAS:
The pancreatic head, body, and tail are visualized without focal mass or ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic fat stranding.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands appear normal in morphology and attenuation. No adrenal nodule or mass to suggest metastatic involvement.

KIDNEYS AND URETERS:
Bilateral kidneys are normal in size and enhancement pattern. No hydronephrosis, calculus, or focal renal mass. Mild cortical thinning bilaterally is noted, compatible with the known history of longstanding hypertension and diabetes mellitus (consistent with chronic nephropathy). No perinephric stranding.

GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT:
The stomach, small bowel, and visualized portions of the colon appear unremarkable. No bowel wall thickening, obstruction, or pneumatosis. No free air. No ascites.

LYMPH NODES - ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:
No retroperitoneal, mesenteric, iliac, or inguinal lymphadenopathy is identified. All visualized nodes are within normal size limits.

PELVIC ORGANS:
Uterus is small in size consistent with postmenopausal status. Ovaries are not distinctly visualized, which is not unusual in a postmenopausal woman. No pelvic free fluid or pelvic mass.

BLADDER:
The urinary bladder is partially distended and appears unremarkable in wall thickness without filling defect.

BONY STRUCTURES:
The imaged bony skeleton, including the spine, ribs, sternum, clavicles, scapulae, pelvis, and visualized proximal long bones, demonstrates no aggressive lytic or sclerotic lesions to suggest osseous metastatic disease. Degenerative changes are present in the lumbar spine (mild facet arthropathy and disc space narrowing at L3-4 and L4-5). Bilateral hip joints show mild joint space narrowing consistent with early degenerative arthropathy. No pathologic fracture.

SOFT TISSUES:
No soft tissue mass identified. Mild subcutaneous fat is present consistent with elevated body habitus (BMI reported as elevated).

VASCULAR:
The aorta and its major branches are patent without aneurysm, dissection, or significant stenosis. Celiac axis, superior and inferior mesenteric arteries patent. No filling defect to suggest pulmonary embolism on this portal venous phase acquisition (dedicated PE protocol not performed).

IMPRESSION:

1. No evidence of distant metastatic disease. The chest, abdomen, and pelvis are clear of pulmonary nodules, hepatic lesions, adrenal masses, retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy, or suspicious osseous lesions.

2. Known left breast primary mass is partially visualized; dedicated breast imaging already performed and on record.

3. Enlarged left axillary lymph node measuring approximately 1.9 cm with loss of fatty hilum, consistent with the previously described suspicious node and locoregional nodal involvement as characterized on the recent breast ultrasound.

4. No mediastinal, hilar, or distant nodal disease identified.

5. Mild bilateral renal cortical thinning, compatible with chronic nephropathy in the setting of longstanding hypertension and diabetes mellitus; no acute renal lesion.

6. Lumbar spine degenerative changes without suspicious bone lesion. No aggressive osseous pathology.

Overall, the findings are consistent with locoregional disease confined to the left breast and ipsilateral axilla, with no CT evidence of systemic metastatic spread. Clinical correlation with bone scan results and multi-disciplinary tumor board review recommended as part of complete staging workup.

Scanning Radiologist: [Attending Radiologist]
Report verified and electronically signed.
